Matrix multiplication with special matrices

LINEAR MATHEMATICS

MATRICES

AUTHOR

Tom Coleman

SUMMARY
Matrices are rectangular arrays of mathematical objects with entries arranged in rows and columns. Matrices are a very useful concept within mathematics and statistics. You’ll see them used for solving simultaneous equations, modelling data in several variables, and much more. This guide will explain how multiplication of special kinds of matrices work.

Before reading this guide, it is strongly recommended that you read Guide: Introduction to matrices and Guide: Matrix multiplication.

As seen in Guide: Matrix multiplication, the idea of matrix multiplication is a key skill in the study of linear mathematics. If A comma B are matrices, the idea for the open paren i comma j close parenth entry of their product A B is to find a ‘product’ of the ith row of A by the jth column of B. This is done by matching entries in the ith row of A to entries in the jth column of B, multiplying the matched entries, and adding them all together.

Mastering matrix multiplication can make your life much easier in many different mathematical aspects. As an example, if you have read Guide: Introduction to solving simultaneous equations then one way of thinking of matrices is as an array encoding the coefficients of the variables of your simultaneous equations. You can then use matrix multiplication to rewrite your simultaneous equations and use matrix methods to solve them: either by row-reduction (see [Guide: Introduction to row-reduction]) or by using matrix inverses (see [Guide: Matrix inverses]). The way this is facilitated is by multiplying a matrix by a vector of variables, which is equal to a vector of answers. This is a special kind of matrix multiplication because of the matrices involved.

This guide looks at matrix multiplication with the special matrices introduced in Guide: Introduction to matrices: vectors, zero and identity matrices, upper- and lower-triangular matrices, and diagonal matrices.

Matrix multiplication with vectors

As you saw in Guide: Introduction to matrices, a matrix with exactly one column and any number of rows is called a column vector, which is often shortened to vector. Vectors are important because they represent positions in co-ordinate systems; they are usually given by lowercase letters in bold fonts bold v (when typed), or underlined v over horizontal bar (when using a pen and paper). See Guide: Introduction to vectors for more.

Example 4 in Guide: Matrix multiplication shows that a 2 times 3 matrix multiplied by a a 3 times 1 vector gives a 2 times 1 vector. This behaviour is true in general:

Note
Product of matrix and a vector

The product of an m times n matrix A with an n times 1 vector bold x is an m times 1 vector bold b.

Tip

What this means is that a m times n matrix maps a point in n-dimensional space to a point in m-dimensional space. You can see this in Example 4; the 2 times 3 matrix A took a point in 3-dimensional space to a point in 2-dimensional space. This is the idea of a matrix acting as a linear transformation; see [Guide: Matrices as linear transformations] for more.

One important application of matrix multiplication of a matrix with a vector is in solving simultaneous equations. This involves multiplying a multiplying a matrix by a vector, bold v, where the entries are variables.

Note
Example 1

Let’s explore the equation A bold x equals bold b for A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 2 Row 2: 3 3 comma bold x equals the 2 by 1 column matrix x y comma bold b equals the 2 by 1 column matrix 5 6 You can look at the product of A and bold x here. Doing this gives A bold x equals the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: 1 times x plus 2 times y Row 2: 3 times x plus 3 times y equals the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: x plus 2 y Row 2: 3 x plus 3 y So the equation A bold x equals bold b becomes the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: x plus 2 y Row 2: 3 x plus 3 y equals the 2 by 1 column matrix 5 6

As two vectors are equal if and only if their entries are equal, the equation A bold x equals bold b is equivalent to the following set of simultaneous equations: 2 lines Line 1: x plus 2 y equals 5 Line 2: 3 x plus 3 y equals 6 You can solve for x and y in A bold x equals bold b, using tools you can learn in Guide: Introduction to solving simultaneous equations. You’ll see this solved in Example 1 of that guide.

Conversely, you can write any set of simultaneous equations as the product A bold x equals bold b where:

A is a matrix orresponding to the coefficients attached to variables of the equations

bold x is a vector of variables

bold b is a vector of answers to the equations.

For instance, the set of simultaneous equations 2 lines Line 1: negative 2 x plus 8 y equals 16 Line 2: x plus 9 y equals negative 8 can be written as A bold x equals bold b where the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 2 8 Row 2: 1 9 over bottom brace over A the 2 by 1 column matrix x y over bottom brace over bold x equals the 2 by 1 column matrix 16 negative 8 over bottom brace over bold b period

Tip

In fact, you can use matrices multiplied by vectors to help find solutions (if they exist) to any number of linear equations in any number of variables. This technique of solving simultaneous equations is based on a set of matrix operations known as row-reduction and you can find out more about it in [Guide: Introduction to row-reduction].

Typically, if you have m many equations in n many variables, then this is represented by an m times n matrix of coefficients multiplying an n times 1 column vector of variables to output an m times 1 column vector of answers.

Let’s see this in action where the matrix isn’t square to begin with:

Note
Example 2

The matrix equation A bold x equals bold b for A equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative 1 2 3 Row 2: 0 3 negative 9 comma bold x equals the 3 by 1 column matrix x y z comma bold b equals the 2 by 1 column matrix negative 1 6 can be written as a system of 2 equations in 3 variables. Evaluating the product gives A bold x equals the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: negative 1 times x plus 2 times y plus 3 times z Row 2: 0 times x plus 3 times y plus negative 9 times z equals the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: negative x plus 2 y plus 3 z Row 2: 3 y minus 9 z So the equation A bold x equals bold b becomes the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: negative x plus 2 y plus 3 z Row 2: 3 y minus 9 z equals the 2 by 1 column matrix negative 1 6

As two vectors are equal if and only if their entries are equal, the equation A bold x equals bold b is equivalent to the following set of simultaneous equations: 2 lines Line 1: negative x plus 2 y plus 3 z equals negative 1 Line 2: 3 y minus 9 z equals 6

You can write any number of linear equations in any number of variables as a matrix product. For instance, the system of linear equations: 3 lines Line 1: x plus 9 y minus 4 z plus t plus w equals 19 Line 2: negative y plus z plus t minus 5 w equals 1 Line 3: negative 8 t minus 8 w equals 0 can be written as A bold x equals bold b where the 3 by 5 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 1 Column 2, 9 Column 3, negative 4 Column 4, 1 Column 5, 1 Row 2: Column 1, 0 Column 2, negative 1 Column 3, 1 Column 4, 1 Column 5, negative 5 Row 3: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 Column 4, negative 8 Column 5, negative 8 over bottom brace over A the 5 by 1 column matrix Row 1: x Row 2: y Row 3: z Row 4: t Row 5: w over bottom brace over bold x equals the 3 by 1 column matrix 19 1 0 over bottom brace over bold b period

You can notice here that for three equations in five variables, the coefficient matrix is represented by a 3 times 5 matrix. This is true in general: for m equations in n variables, the coefficient matrix is an m times n matrix.

Matrix multiplication with zero and identity matrices

You saw in Guide: Introduction to matrices that two other special kinds of matrices are

m times n zero matrices 0 sub m times n where all the entries in an m times n matrix are 0, and

n times n identity matrices I sub n where the entries in an n times n matrix are 1’s down the main diagonal and 0’s everywhere else.

These matrices play the same role respectively that the numbers 0 and 1 do in multiplication of numbers. Where the multiplication is defined, you’d expect any matrix multiplied by a zero matrix to equal another zero matrix, and any matrix multiplied by an identity matrix to not change at all. This is the exact behaviour that happens:

Note
Matrix multiplication with zero and identity matrices

Let A be any m times n matrix.

If 0 sub p times m is the p times m zero matrix, then 0 sub p times m A equals 0 sub p times n is the p times n zero matrix. Similarly, if 0 sub n times q is the n times q zero matrix, then A 0 sub n times q A equals 0 sub m times q is the m times q zero matrix.

If I sub m is the m times m identity matrix, then I sub m A equals A. Similarly, if I sub n is the n times n identity matrix, then A I sub n equals A.

For explanations as to why these results are true, please see [Proof sheet: Properties of matrix arithmetic].

Warning

Although it is true that the product of any matrix with a zero matrix is a zero matrix, it’s not true that if the product of two matrices is a zero matrix, then at least one of the matrices is a zero matrix. See Example 7 for more on this.

Note
Example 3

Let A be the following 2 times 3 matrix: A equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 2 negative 1 Row 2: 4 0 3

It follows that if 0 sub 4 times 2 is the 4 times 2 zero matrix, then 3 lines Line 1: 0 sub 4 times 2 A equals the 4 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Row 2: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Row 3: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Row 4: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 times the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 2 negative 1 Row 2: 4 0 3 Line 2: blank equals the 4 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 times 0 plus 0 times 4 Column 2, 0 times 2 plus 0 times 0 Column 3, 0 times negative 1 plus 0 times 3 Row 2: Column 1, 0 times 0 plus 0 times 4 Column 2, 0 times 2 plus 0 times 0 Column 3, 0 times negative 1 plus 0 times 3 Row 3: Column 1, 0 times 0 plus 0 times 4 Column 2, 0 times 2 plus 0 times 0 Column 3, 0 times negative 1 plus 0 times 3 Row 4: Column 1, 0 times 0 plus 0 times 4 Column 2, 0 times 2 plus 0 times 0 Column 3, 0 times negative 1 plus 0 times 3 Line 3: blank equals the 4 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 Row 2: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 Row 3: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 Row 4: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 equals 0 sub 4 times 3

But you absolutely don’t have to write out all of the working for this! It is enough to write something like A 0 sub 3 times 8 equals 0 sub 2 times 8 which saves you from writing out at least 42 zeroes - and more if you show working!

If I sub 3 is the 3 times 3 identity matrix, then

3 lines Line 1: A I sub 3 equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 2 negative 1 Row 2: 4 0 3 times the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 1 0 0 Row 2: 0 1 0 Row 3: 0 0 1 Line 2: blank equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 times 1 plus 2 times 0 plus negative 1 times 0 Column 2, 0 times 0 plus 2 times 1 plus negative 1 times 0 Column 3, 0 times 0 plus 2 times 0 plus negative 1 times 1 Row 2: Column 1, 4 times 1 plus 0 times 0 plus 3 times 0 Column 2, 4 times 0 plus 0 times 1 plus 3 times 0 Column 3, 4 times 0 plus 0 times 0 plus 3 times 1 Line 3: blank equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 2 negative 1 Row 2: 4 0 3 equals A

Matrix multiplication with other special matrices

The other kinds of special matrices that you saw in Guide: Introduction to matrices were square matrices, upper triangular and lower triangular matrices, and diagonal matrices.

These are special because all of these types of matrices are said to be closed under matrix multiplication. Unlike general matrix multiplication, which could have matrices of three different dimensions involved in a product, because all of these types of matrices are square this is not a problem.

Here’s the lowdown.

Note
Matrix multiplication with other special matrices

Let A comma B be any two n times n square matrices.

The products A B and B A are defined, and both A B and B A are n times n square matrices.

The matrix power A to the n-th power equals A times A times A times dot dot dot times A over bottom brace over n times exists and is an n times n square matrix.

If both A comma B are upper triangular matrices, then both A B and B A are upper triangular matrices.

If both A comma B are lower triangular matrices, then both A B and B A are lower triangular matrices.

If both A comma B are diagonal matrices, then both A B and B A are diagonal matrices.

This next example shows part (d) of this statement in detail.

Note
Example 4

Let A comma B comma C be the following 3 times 3 diagonal matrices: A equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 0 0 Row 2: 0 negative 1 0 Row 3: 0 0 negative 1 comma B equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 1 0 0 Row 2: 0 2 0 Row 3: 0 0 3 sans serif and B equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 5 0 0 Row 2: 0 0 0 Row 3: 0 0 0

Then

3 lines Line 1: A B equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 0 0 Row 2: 0 negative 1 0 Row 3: 0 0 negative 1 times the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 1 0 0 Row 2: 0 2 0 Row 3: 0 0 3 Line 2: blank equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 times 1 plus 0 times 0 plus 0 times 0 Column 2, 0 times 0 plus 0 times 2 plus 0 times 0 Column 3, 0 times 1 plus 0 times 0 plus 0 times 3 Row 2: Column 1, 0 times 1 plus negative 1 times 0 plus 0 times 0 Column 2, 0 times 0 plus negative 1 times 2 plus 0 times 0 Column 3, 0 times 0 plus negative 1 times 0 plus 0 times 3 Row 3: Column 1, 0 times 1 plus 0 times 0 plus negative 1 times 0 Column 2, 0 times 0 plus 0 times 2 plus negative 1 times 0 Column 3, 0 times 0 plus 0 times 0 plus negative 1 times 3 Line 3: blank equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 times 1 Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 Row 2: Column 1, 0 Column 2, negative 1 times 2 Column 3, 0 Row 3: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, negative 1 times 3 equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 0 0 Row 2: 0 negative 2 0 Row 3: 0 0 negative 3 Here, you can notice the product of A B is the diagonal matrix where each main diagonal entry in A B is the product of the corresponding diagonal entries from A and B. In fact, this is true in general, for any two n times n diagonal matrices.

You can apply this to find out the product of matrices A C:

A C equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 0 0 Row 2: 0 negative 1 0 Row 3: 0 0 negative 1 times the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 5 0 0 Row 2: 0 0 0 Row 3: 0 0 0 equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 times 5 Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 Row 2: Column 1, 0 Column 2, negative 1 times 0 Column 3, 0 Row 3: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, negative 1 times 0 equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 0 0 Row 2: 0 0 0 Row 3: 0 0 0 This shows that there are two non-zero matrices that multiply together to make a zero matrix!

Finally, finding powers of diagonal matrices can be obtained by finding the corresponding powers of its entries. For instance

A to the fourth power equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 0 0 Row 2: 0 negative 1 0 Row 3: 0 0 negative 1 to the fourth power equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 to the fourth power Column 2, 0 Column 3, 0 Row 2: Column 1, 0 Column 2, open paren negative 1 close paren to the fourth power Column 3, 0 Row 3: Column 1, 0 Column 2, 0 Column 3, open paren negative 1 close paren to the fourth power equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 0 0 Row 2: 0 1 0 Row 3: 0 0 1 equals negative A period

Note
Multiplying diagonal matrices

Let A comma B be two n times n diagonal matrices with sans serif diag A equals open paren a sub 11 comma a sub 22 comma dot dot dot comma a sub n n close paren and sans serif diag B equals open paren b sub 11 comma b sub 22 comma dot dot dot comma b sub n n close paren. Then A B is an n times n diagonal matrix with sans serif diag A B equals open paren a sub 11 b sub 11 comma a sub 22 b sub 22 comma dot dot dot comma a sub n n b sub n n close paren.

Warning

If two matrices A B multiply together to make some zero matrix, this does not guarantee that either of A or B is a zero matrix!

Tip

As you have seen, multiplying together diagonal matrices is computationally far easier than multiplying together general matrices; compare Example 2 to Example 7! In fact, this is one of the reasons behind the mathematical technique of diagonalizing a square matrix: see [Guide: Introduction to matrix diagonalization] for more.

Quick check problems

Decide whether the following statements are true or false.

If A is an n times n diagonal matrix, then A I sub n is a diagonal matrix. Answer: 
TRUE
FALSE
 .

If A is an m times n matrix and double struck x is an m times 1 column vector, then A bold x is an n times 1 column vector. Answer: 
TRUE
FALSE
 .

The product of an n times n upper-triangular matrix with an n times p zero matrix gives another upper-triangular matrix. Answer 
TRUE
FALSE

Find the product bold A bold v where A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 3 0 Row 2: 4 negative 2 and bold v equals the 2 by 1 column matrix 1 3 period

Answer: The entry open bracket A bold v close bracket sub 11 equals  and open bracket A bold v close bracket sub 21 equals  .

You are given the following equation the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 1 1 negative 1 Row 2: 0 2 0 Row 3: 0 0 1 times the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 3 1 0 Row 2: 0 1 negative 2 Row 3: 0 0 negative 1 equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 3 a negative 1 Row 2: b 2 negative 4 Row 3: c d e Find a comma b comma c comma d comma e.

Answer: a equals  , b equals  , c equals  , d equals  , e equals

Further reading

For more questions on this topic, please go to Questions: Matrix multiplication with special matrices.

For more about the definition of matrix multiplication, please go to Guide: Matrix multiplication.
